narrative: As a white male applicant with a full-time study plan, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 20, lower-middle family income bracket, and low deciles in Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—and predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The decision hinged most on three key factors from your background, ranked by their influence on this outcome. First, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the typical student's average of around 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers. Second, your LSAT score of 20, which is notably lower than the average of 37 seen in the dataset, also significantly contributed to the failure prediction by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness essential for bar success. Third, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, as students from similar backgrounds have historically shown resilience in overcoming challenges. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 was slightly below average and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these played lesser roles here. Overall, your profile stands out as riskier than typical students, who tend to have stronger mid-range deciles, higher LSATs, and more balanced backgrounds leading to pass predictions. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your determination—many factors like personal growth aren't fully captured—but a data-driven call to protect the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or targeted improvement, and exploring programs with bar prep support or financial aid suited to your income level. With focused effort on these areas, you could reshape your profile for future applications and build the skills needed to pass the bar confidently.
